title: Why Banning Under-16s from Social Media Will Do More Harm Than Good
content:
In December, Australia stopped under-16s from having accounts on platforms including Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok. A dozen other countries, including Britain and Spain, are now toying with the idea of similar bans. More than 70 percent of Britons and two-thirds of Americans support kicking young teenagers off such sites. These proposals arise from an understandable desire to keep youngsters safe and healthy. Parents have been deeply shaken by tragedies where social media played a direct role in harming children.

However, many experts argue that blanket bans will ultimately do more harm than good. The question of whether social media causes mass harm is still far from settled among researchers. While growing evidence suggests it can be bad for some children, claims of universal damage lack conclusive proof. Enforcing these bans is also notoriously difficult, with teenagers quickly finding ingenious ways to bypass age restrictions. Furthermore, defining what constitutes social media is tricky, often leaving loopholes for messaging apps and multiplayer games.

Kids barred from mainstream sites might flock to more obscure, unmoderated corners of the internet. Children who evade blocks may also be less likely to report horrid experiences to adults out of fear. Instead of blanket bans, regulators should demand that tech giants rethink addictive features like endless scrolling. They must enforce sterner moderation and require platforms to provide data for researchers to measure actual harm. Ultimately, adults must work to make young people's time online as safe and rewarding as possible.
